title: Scientific advances ready to change the world: the Top 10 Emerging Technologies 2026
gist: The WEF's 14th annual Top 10 Emerging Technologies report (2026) identifies ten near-commercial technologies — spanning grid energy, lithium extraction, cooling materials, forever-chemical destruction, precision fermentation, exosome drug delivery, personalised mRNA cancer vaccines, quantum drug simulation, AI world models, and lattice-based cryptography — each expected to achieve material real-world impact within five years. A cross-cutting meta-theme is that AI is restructuring the scientific method itself, enabling pre-hypothesis experimentation at scale and compressing the path from discovery to clinical or commercial deployment. Supply chain localisation, hyper-personalisation of medicine, and 'doing more with less' (cooling without electricity, lithium without desert geography) are identified as the unifying macro vectors.

insights.claim: Everything-to-grid (E2G) energy converts buildings, EVs, rooftop solar panels, and warehouse batteries from passive consumers into bidirectional nodes that stabilise the grid in real time, fundamentally reimagining grid architecture rather than just adding capacity.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Long-term bullish for smart-grid software (SCADA, demand-response platforms), vehicle-to-grid hardware makers, and next-gen battery chemistry companies; challenges traditional utility capex models and centralised grid operators.
insights.quote: Buildings, vehicles, and devices are no longer just electricity consumers. They are now active resources that can help reimagine the grid.

insights.claim: AI-driven energy demand from data centres is arriving simultaneously with climate-driven grid stress, creating an urgent structural case for distributed grid management technologies beyond conventional grid upgrades.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: AI infrastructure buildout is a direct demand driver for grid stabilisation tech — creates a dual-catalyst investment thesis for grid software, bidirectional inverters, and distributed energy resource management systems (DERMS).
insights.quote: We're starting to hear conversations about the energy demands of AI. And so when we're already at a point where grids can be really stressed by increasing temperatures and we have this new force coming in, it's really important that we start to think about how might we reimagine that grid.

insights.claim: New battery chemistries (lithium and sodium) enabling bidirectional grid-scale storage, new wide-bandgap semiconductors, and new control systems allowing distributed storage to actively stabilise rather than passively feed grids are the three enabling hardware layers for E2G.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Three investable hardware categories in parallel: battery chemistry companies, power semiconductor fabs, and grid-edge control software. Watch for cross-over bets that address all three.

insights.claim: E2G energy benefits skew toward property-owning, vehicle-owning households; renters and non-car owners risk being excluded from value capture, creating a distributional equity challenge that could attract regulatory intervention.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Policy risk is real for E2G scale-up: regulators may mandate landlord pass-through or utility tariff reform, creating uncertainty for business models. Monitor EU and US utility commission rulings on VPP compensation structures.

insights.claim: Direct lithium extraction (DLE) can pull lithium from brine in hours versus up to two years for conventional evaporation-pond methods, is modular, returns water to the source aquifer, and eliminates the need for high-altitude desert geology — breaking the geographic concentration of supply.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Structurally bearish for Chilean/Argentinian lithium brine majors and for the evaporation-pond supply chain; bullish for DLE tech companies and for battery manufacturers that could co-locate extraction and refining to collapse the supply chain.

insights.claim: Current lithium refining is dominated by China at 62% of global capacity, with Chile at 13% and Argentina at 11%; the entire supply chain involves multi-continent movement — extraction in South America, refining in China, battery manufacturing in the US or Europe.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: DLE's modularity could allow co-located extraction and refining, directly challenging China's refining dominance and creating reshoring opportunities — strategically significant for US/EU critical minerals policy and battery supply chain security.

insights.claim: Lithium prices have fallen over 80% since their 2022 peak, creating a commodity investment paradox: lower prices reduce incentive to build new extraction plants at exactly the moment when new DLE capacity is most needed.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Classic resource-cycle value destruction — DLE adoption may be delayed by commodity price headwinds even as long-term demand signals are bullish. Investors should watch for government offtake agreements or strategic stockpile purchases that could bridge the financing gap.
insights.quote: Lithium has lost over 80% of its value since 2022 and that makes so why would I then invest in a new plant making this? And that's a challenge to scale.

insights.claim: Passive radiative cooling (PRC) materials — paints, roof coatings, window films, and fabrics — can reduce building temperatures below ambient air temperature without using any electricity by emitting heat directly into deep space, bypassing the atmosphere.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Large addressable market across both new construction and retrofits; uniquely suited to lower-income, high-heat geographies where air conditioning is unaffordable — creates a development finance and impact investment opportunity alongside commercial real estate plays.

insights.claim: Urban heat islands are already 0.5 to 4 degrees Celsius warmer than surrounding rural areas on average; passive radiative cooling applied at city scale could deliver up to 40% energy savings from cooling load reduction.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: 40% cooling-energy savings is a significant load-reduction signal for utilities and grid operators; also a powerful ROI metric to drive building-material specification and municipal procurement. Translates into avoided capex on peaker plant capacity.
insights.quote: Energy savings using passive radiative cooling materials, according to this report, could reach up to 40%.

insights.claim: PRC has a critical non-residential application in industrial supply chains — keeping factories operational in high-heat locations previously unviable, and extending the geographic range of cold-chain logistics without added power draw.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Industrial and logistics real estate developers in heat-stressed emerging markets (Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa) should factor PRC into capex decisions; potential risk mitigation for supply-chain insurers.

insights.claim: A UK company has developed a PRC coating for power cables that keeps them cool enough to carry more electricity, enabling denser current capacity on existing cable infrastructure without replacement.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Potential to defer expensive cable replacement capex for utilities — watch for grid operator procurement trials. Could also serve as an enabling technology for high-density EV charging corridors.

insights.claim: PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) — based on the carbon-fluorine bond, one of the strongest in organic chemistry — have been detected in Arctic environments, in rainwater on every continent, and in the bloodstream of almost all tested humans; historical approaches have only contained them, not destroyed them.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Regulatory and liability exposure is becoming systemic across industries (firefighting, nonstick coatings, textiles, semiconductors); PFAS remediation technology is moving from niche government contract work toward broader industrial and municipal applications.
insights.quote: We've found PFAS in places like the Arctic, it's in rainwater on every continent in the world, and even actually in the bloodstream of almost everyone we've tested for it.

insights.claim: Multiple technical approaches now exist to break the carbon-fluorine bond, including electrochemical methods that strip PFAS molecules from contaminated water using specialised electrodes; the technology is transitioning from research to early development, primarily government-funded.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Government funding pipeline is the primary near-term revenue driver; investors should watch EPA and EU remediation mandate timelines. Brownfield land unlocked by PFAS destruction represents a significant secondary real estate value play.

insights.claim: PFAS-contaminated land previously deemed untouchable for investment could be commercially repurposed once destruction technology matures, creating a wave of brownfield redevelopment opportunity.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Long-dated optionality in contaminated real estate near former industrial or military sites; value creation contingent on regulatory push and cost of remediation falling to commercial viability — timeline likely 5-10 years post technology validation.

insights.claim: Precision fermentation uses reprogrammed microbes (e.g., yeast) to produce proteins, fats, and other molecules — including whey protein and egg proteins — chemically identical to animal-derived equivalents, without requiring farmland, livestock, or traditional agricultural inputs.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Direct disruption threat to conventional dairy and egg producers; bullish for bioreactor manufacturers and fermentation ingredient companies. Walmart partnership cited as early proof of mainstream retail distribution.

insights.claim: By 2050, a global population of 9 billion will require food production at a scale that the current agricultural system — already at or near planetary limits for land use, water use, and emissions — mathematically cannot deliver using existing methods.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Structural demand signal for alternative protein and food-tech sectors over a 25-year horizon; macro underpinning for precision fermentation, vertical farming, and lab-grown meat investment theses.
insights.quote: By 2050 we'll have a world population of nine billion people and we're going to have to feed them all. The way we do that right now, the math doesn't work to scale that.

insights.claim: Precision fermentation outputs extend beyond food — chemicals previously derived from fossil fuels and cosmetic ingredients can be produced through the same fermentation process, creating a broader industrial biotechnology market.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Upside optionality extends well beyond food markets; precision fermentation companies with platform chemistry capabilities could disrupt petrochemical intermediates — watch for cross-sector licensing deals.

insights.claim: The main social risk of precision fermentation is displacement of generations of cattle ranchers and agricultural communities — particularly in less developed economies where farming is the primary livelihood — representing a structural disruption risk requiring deliberate policy management.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: ESG and political risk for rapid precision fermentation scale-up; governments in agricultural economies (Brazil, Australia, New Zealand) may impose defensive regulations or tariffs on lab-produced proteins, creating market access risk.

insights.claim: Exosome drug delivery exploits the body's own cell-to-cell messaging vesicles (exosomes) as couriers for therapeutic payloads; because exosomes are endogenous structures, the body does not reject them, and they can cross previously impenetrable biological barriers including the blood-brain barrier.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Opens pharmacological access to CNS diseases (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's) and hard-to-treat cancers (pancreatic) that have resisted treatment for decades — creates significant long-term value for companies developing exosome delivery platforms and for neurodegenerative disease drug pipelines.
insights.quote: You load the packet with therapeutic cargo. And then the body accepts the delivery because it recognises the courier.

insights.claim: Clinical trials of exosome drug delivery have shown efficacy in treating pancreatic cancer, neurological diseases, and long-COVID effects; exosomes can cross the blood-brain barrier that has historically blocked treatment for Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Early clinical evidence in pancreatic cancer is particularly notable given the disease's notoriously poor treatment response; watch for Phase 2/3 readouts from exosome-delivery biotechs as potential re-rating catalysts.

insights.claim: Personalised mRNA cancer vaccines can sequence a patient's specific tumour mutations from a biopsy and manufacture a bespoke vaccine targeting that exact cancer within weeks, replacing the mass-production blockbuster chemotherapy model with per-patient precision treatment.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Structural disruption to the pharmaceutical industry's blockbuster scale model — large-cap pharma with heavy chemotherapy portfolios face long-term revenue erosion; bullish for mRNA platform companies and for decentralised oncology lab-in-hospital infrastructure.

insights.claim: Early personalised mRNA cancer vaccine treatments exceed $100,000 per patient, placing them well beyond reach for most of the global population; scaling equitable access will require health system reform and hybrid personalised/off-the-shelf models to reduce per-patient cost.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Near-term revenues confined to high-income healthcare systems (US, EU, Japan); long-term market expansion requires cost reduction from AI-assisted manufacturing and process automation — watch for announcements on AI-assisted antigen design platforms.
insights.quote: Early treatments exceeding $100,000 per patient put personalised vaccines well within reach of patients.

insights.claim: AI could accelerate the cost reduction of personalised mRNA vaccines by automating parts of the discovery and manufacturing process, enabling the localisation of production to the same building where patients are treated rather than centralised pharmaceutical plants.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Hospital-adjacent biomanufacturing is a potential new real estate and infrastructure category; creates demand for modular bioreactor hardware and AI-driven antigen identification software at clinical site level.

insights.claim: Quantum simulation models drug candidate molecules atom-by-atom using the laws of quantum physics rather than classical approximations, allowing researchers to observe how a molecule folds and locks to its target before synthesising a single molecule in the lab.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Represents a step-change in drug R&D efficiency — the nearest-term practical application for quantum computing with verifiable commercial ROI, making pharma partnerships the most credible near-term revenue path for quantum hardware/software companies.
insights.quote: Conventional computers approximate molecular behaviour by reducing its complexity. Quantum simulation models it directly.
insights.locator: Technology #8: Quantum simulation for drug discovery

insights.claim: Nine out of ten drug candidates globally fail clinical trials; a significant contributing factor is that conventional computational modelling relies on molecular approximations rather than direct simulation, leading to costly late-stage failures.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: If quantum simulation shifts even the failure rate from 9-in-10 to 7-in-10, the economic impact across pharma R&D would be enormous — quantify: global drug R&D spend ~$250B/year; even a 20% efficiency gain is a $50B productivity prize. Bullish for quantum software layer companies targeting pharma.
insights.quote: Nine in 10 drug candidates around the world fail trials.
insights.locator: Technology #8: Quantum simulation for drug discovery

insights.claim: Quantum simulation could open the economic case for treating rare diseases — where small patient populations have historically made drug development commercially unviable — by collapsing discovery costs for diseases with no current pharmaceutical investment.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Potential new category of addressable market for pharma and biotech — hundreds of millions of patients with rare diseases globally currently underserved. Could also attract government procurement and rare disease fund investment.
insights.locator: Technology #8: Quantum simulation for drug discovery

insights.claim: World models — AI systems trained on multi-modal sensory data (video, depth sensors, pressure, motion capture) rather than text — learn causal physical relationships from experience, enabling robots, climate models, and autonomous systems to respond to ambiguous real-world situations rather than executing rigid pre-programmed actions.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Closes the gap between language-capable AI and physically-embodied AI — near-term applications in factory automation (flexible manufacturing robots), autonomous vehicles, and climate forecasting, all areas with large invested capital bases that would benefit from this capability.
insights.quote: The gap between what AI can learn and what it can actually understand about the world is starting to close.

insights.claim: Current large language models learn from descriptions of the world in text, while world models learn from direct physical experience — the distinction enables factory robots that can adapt to novel floor conditions, climate models that can understand storm dynamics, and manufacturing plants that self-optimise during operation.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Distinct investment category from the current LLM/RAG stack — world model development requires different data infrastructure (sensor fusion, simulation environments) and hardware (edge compute, robotics sensors) than language-model scaling.

insights.claim: Autonomous vehicle fleets in cities like San Francisco are generating real-world physical interaction data (sensor fusion, multi-modal perception) that will likely feed world model training corpora — making AV data a strategic competitive input to physical AI development.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: AV companies' proprietary sensor data libraries may have embedded option value as training feedstock for world models — a non-obvious asset that could be monetised via licensing or creates moat advantages for vertically integrated physical AI companies.

insights.claim: Lattice-based cryptography hides data within a high-dimensional mathematical grid with embedded random noise, creating a 'mathematical fog' that even quantum computers cannot efficiently penetrate — making it the leading candidate for post-quantum encryption standards.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Every enterprise and government IT system in the world will need to migrate to post-quantum encryption before quantum computers capable of breaking current RSA/ECC encryption are deployed — creates a multi-year, mandatory infrastructure upgrade cycle comparable to Y2K or IPv6 migration.

insights.claim: Adversaries are already executing 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks — collecting encrypted data today with the intent to decrypt it once quantum computers of sufficient power exist, meaning sensitive data captured today is at risk of exposure in 5-10 years.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: The threat is not hypothetical or future — it is active today. This creates an immediate and urgent business case for enterprise post-quantum migration, particularly for long-lived sensitive data (government, financial, health records). Security vendors with PQC offerings are beneficiaries now.
insights.quote: Criminals can have a harvest now, decrypt later mentality, which means they can get all this decrypted data... if they just hold it for a few years, they'll have a computer, strong enough because it will be a quantum computer, that it will be able to decrypt it.

insights.claim: The US NIST has already established lattice-based cryptography as the core of its quantum-safe computing standards, signalling regulatory convergence that will mandate migration across US government contractors and likely cascade to allied nations and regulated industries.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: NIST standardisation is the key catalyst for enterprise procurement — government contractors are first movers, followed by financial services, healthcare, and critical infrastructure. Creates a near-term revenue pipeline for PQC software and HSM (hardware security module) vendors.
insights.quote: In the United States, lattice-based cryptography is kind of the core to the big quantum safe computing standards that have been put in place.

insights.claim: Homomorphic encryption enabled by lattice-based cryptography allows computation on encrypted data without decrypting it — demonstrated in a case where AI was trained across 300,000 patient records from three hospitals without exposing any underlying individual patient data.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Unlocks data-sharing collaboration across institutions (hospitals, banks, sovereign data holders) that currently cannot share due to privacy and regulatory constraints — creates an entirely new category of permissioned data infrastructure and federated AI training markets.

insights.claim: Technologies fail to scale not because of weak underlying science but due to three predictable bottlenecks: (1) missing enabling infrastructure (e.g., mRNA lacked a delivery system from its 2014 identification until lipid nanoparticles emerged in 2018); (2) no first mover willing to accept the cost and risk of early adoption; (3) absence of policy or regulatory mandate to drive demand.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Actionable framework for technology risk assessment: investors should evaluate each technology against all three bottlenecks, not just scientific readiness. A strong scientific case with missing infrastructure or absent first-mover is a mispriced optionality situation, not a near-term investment.
insights.quote: For the most part, the reason isn't the technology. The research is there and really strong... It tends to come down to three things. The first is whether the pieces around it are ready... The second factor... is whether there's someone who's ready to take that first bet... Other times we see government creating that push.

insights.claim: mRNA technology was identified in WEF's 2014 Top 10 Emerging Technologies report but required until 2018 for lipid nanoparticle delivery systems to emerge before it could scale — a four-year gap between scientific identification and enabling infrastructure that ultimately led to the 2020-21 COVID vaccine breakthrough.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the multi-year lag between WEF technology identification and commercial/clinical readiness; the 2026 list should be treated as a 5-10 year investment horizon, not a 12-18 month catalyst calendar.
insights.quote: We first identified mRNA in 2014. The science was solid. But there was no good way to get the mRNA into human cells. The missing piece was the delivery system, and that didn't come around until 2018.

insights.claim: South Australia's government-backed grid battery — deployed following a state power crisis — served as the critical first-mover event that catalysed global grid-scale battery storage adoption, demonstrating how government-crisis-driven procurement can unlock commercial markets for immature technologies.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Government crisis-response procurement is a recurring catalyst for stranded-technology commercialisation — identify energy, climate, or security crises in major economies that could serve as South Australia-style trigger events for technologies on the 2026 list.

insights.claim: AI is restructuring the scientific method itself: traditionally, science proceeds hypothesis → experiment → learning; AI enables pre-hypothesis experimentation — asking broad open questions, generating hypotheses from exploratory data runs, and then using physical experiments as validation rather than discovery.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Compresses drug discovery timelines, materials science iteration cycles, and basic research by eliminating hypothesis-formation bottleneck; creates structural demand for compute, specialised foundation models trained on scientific literature and experimental data, and AI-native research lab tooling.
insights.quote: With AI, what we're able to do is do a bit of experimentation pre-hypothesis, we're able to ask really, really broad open questions and develop our hypothesis from that so that then typically the experiment phase becomes a bit more of a validation stage. It really shifts the way science is happening.
insights.locator: Closing discussion: AI and the future of science

insights.claim: The 2026 technology cohort reflects three cross-cutting macro shifts: (1) hyper-personalisation of health and energy services (personalised vaccines, distributed E2G); (2) supply chain localisation (co-located lithium extraction/refining, city-based protein bioreactors, neighbourhood-level energy balancing); (3) 'doing more with less' — delivering outcomes without proportional additional resource consumption (cooling without electricity, lithium without desert geography).
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: These three vectors are investable macro themes that cut across multiple sectors: personalisation favours platform companies over mass-market incumbents; localisation favours distributed infrastructure over centralised production; resource efficiency favours asset-light, chemistry-driven companies.

insights.claim: The WEF has published this report for 14 years, creating a validated backtesting corpus; technologies that have scaled successfully show that neither great science nor great timing alone is sufficient — the convergence of enabling infrastructure, first-mover willingness, and policy support is required.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Investors can retrospectively validate the three-factor scaling model against the 14-year archive — any current-list technology showing all three green lights (enabling infrastructure in place, first mover identified, policy tailwind) is highest-probability for near-term commercialisation.
